curriculum_source: CFA Program 2027 Level III Volume 2, Portfolio Construction, Learning Module 6

drills.los_text: explain the components of execution costs, including explicit and implicit costs

options.A: direct costs of trading — broker commission costs, transaction taxes, stamp duties, and fees paid to exchanges.
options.B: market impact only.
options.C: bid-ask spread and delay.

rationales.A:
Full statement: direct costs of trading — broker commission costs, transaction taxes, stamp duties, and fees paid to exchanges; costs for which a trader could receive a receipt.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Explicit costs are the direct costs of trading, such as broker commission costs, transaction taxes, stamp duties, and fees paid to exchanges. They are costs for which a trader could receive a receipt.' Explicit costs are visible and directly measurable via invoices.
rationales.B: Market impact is implicit.
rationales.C: Both are implicit.

options.A: commissions.
options.B: (1) bid-ask spread, (2) market impact/price impact, (3) delay costs/slippage, (4) opportunity costs/unrealized P&L.
options.C: taxes.

rationales.A: Commissions are explicit.
rationales.B:
Full statement: (1) bid-ask spread, (2) market impact/price impact, (3) delay costs/slippage, (4) opportunity costs/unrealized P&L — indirect costs caused by the market impact of trading and price concessions impatient traders make to complete trades.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Implicit costs, by contrast, are indirect costs caused by the market impact of trading... Implicit costs result from the following issues: The bid–ask spread... Market impact (or price impact) is the effect of the trade on transaction prices... Delay costs (also called slippage) arise from the inability to complete the desired trade immediately... Opportunity costs (or unrealized profit/loss) arise from the failure to execute a trade promptly.' The four components: spread, market impact, delay, and opportunity cost.
rationales.C: Taxes are explicit.

options.A: The bid-ask spread is the mid quote price between the standing bid and ask.
options.B: The bid-ask spread is the discount rate dealers apply when financing inventory positions.
options.C: the ask price minus the bid price — the price at which a trader will sell a specified quantity minus the price at which a trader will buy a specified quantity.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The midpoint is a reference PRICE; the spread is the DIFFERENCE — ask minus bid.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Financing rates are a separate dealer economics item; the spread is the sell-price-minus-buy-price gap.
rationales.C:
Full statement: the ask price minus the bid price — the price at which a trader will sell a specified quantity minus the price at which a trader will buy a specified quantity; traders who want to trade quickly buy at higher prices and sell at lower prices than those willing to wait.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The bid–ask spread is the ask price (the price at which a trader will sell a specified quantity of a security) minus the bid price (the price at which a trader will buy a specified quantity of a security). Traders who want to trade quickly buy at higher prices and sell at lower prices than those willing to wait for others to trade with them.'

options.A: is the effect of the trade on transaction prices — traders who want to fill large orders often must move prices to encourage others to trade with them.
options.B: Market impact affects only small trades, since large orders are negotiated privately.
options.C: Market impact is always zero in liquid markets with continuous quoting.

rationales.A:
Full statement: is the effect of the trade on transaction prices — traders who want to fill large orders often must move prices to encourage others to trade with them; small market orders have small market impact because filled by traders willing to trade at quoted prices; larger orders require larger price concessions.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Market impact (or price impact) is the effect of the trade on transaction prices. Traders who want to fill large orders often must move prices to encourage others to trade with them... For small orders, market impact often is limited to buying at bid prices and selling at lower ask prices. Small market orders generally have small market impact because these orders often are immediately filled by traders willing to trade at quoted bid and offer prices, or even better prices. Larger orders have greater market impact when traders must move the market to fill their orders.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Impact grows WITH size — large orders must move prices to draw out counterparties.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Impact is a real, measurable cost even in the most liquid markets.

options.A: Delay costs arise from the commissions brokers charge for handling orders.
options.B: arise from the inability to complete the desired trade immediately — traders fail to profit when they fill their orders after prices move as they expect.
options.C: Delay costs are always incurred immediately at the moment the order is submitted.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Commissions are explicit costs; delay costs arise from the INABILITY to trade immediately.
rationales.B:
Full statement: arise from the inability to complete the desired trade immediately — traders fail to profit when they fill their orders after prices move as they expect; often measured on the portion of the order carried over from one day to the next; delay is costly when price moves away from an order (up for buy, down for sell), often because information leaks into the market.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Delay costs (also called slippage) arise from the inability to complete the desired trade immediately... Effective spreads also do not measure delay costs (also called slippage) that arise from the inability to complete the desired trade immediately because of its size in relation to the available market liquidity. Delay costs also arise when portfolio managers or their traders fail to create and route orders quickly to the markets where they will fill most quickly. Analysts often measure delay costs on the portion of the order carried over from one day to the next. Delay is costly when price moves away from an order (up for a buy order, down for a sell order), often because information leaks into the market.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The cost accrues as prices move away DURING the delay — filling after prices have run.

options.A: Opportunity costs arise from executions that complete successfully at favorable prices.
options.B: Opportunity costs are another name for broker commissions.
options.C: the failure to execute a trade promptly — traders fail to profit when their orders fail to trade and prices move as they expect.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Successful fills incur no opportunity cost — the cost belongs to orders that FAIL to trade.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Commissions are paid on trades done; opportunity cost is profit missed when unfilled orders would have paid off.
rationales.C:
Full statement: the failure to execute a trade promptly — traders fail to profit when their orders fail to trade and prices move as they expect; often significant for large orders; when delays cause a portion of the order to go unfilled, associated cost is opportunity cost.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Opportunity costs (or unrealized profit/loss) arise from the failure to execute a trade promptly. Traders fail to profit when their orders fail to trade and price move as they expect... When delays in execution cause a portion of the order to go unfilled, the associated cost is called opportunity cost.' Opportunity costs are difficult to measure — sensitive to time frame chosen and assumptions about prices at which orders could have traded.

questions.stem: For *buy-side institutions*, *fixed trading costs* *most likely* include:

options.A: costs of employing buy-side traders, costs of equipping them with proper trading tools (electronic systems and data), costs of office space (trading rooms or corners).
options.B: Buy-side fixed trading costs consist of commissions only.
options.C: Buy-side institutions bear no fixed trading costs of any kind.

rationales.A:
Full statement: costs of employing buy-side traders, costs of equipping them with proper trading tools (electronic systems and data), costs of office space (trading rooms or corners); small buy-side institutions often avoid these by not employing buy-side traders and having portfolio managers submit orders directly to brokers.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The costs of trading include fixed costs and variable costs. For buy-side institutions, fixed trading costs include the costs of employing buy-side traders, the costs of equipping them with proper trading tools (electronic systems and data), and the costs of office space (trading rooms or corners). Small buy-side institutions often avoid these costs by not employing buy-side traders. Their portfolio managers submit their orders directly to their brokers. Variable transaction costs arise from trading activity and consist of explicit and implicit costs.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Commissions are VARIABLE per-trade costs; the fixed base is people, systems, data, and space.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Employing traders, equipping them with electronic systems and data, and housing them are real fixed costs.

questions.stem: The *limit order book* for ECSI example shows Dealer A bid 98.85/600 shares ask 100.51/1,000; Dealer B bid 98.84/500 ask 100.55/500; Dealer C bid 98.82/700 ask 100.49/200. The *market spread* is *most likely*:

options.A: 1.66 (Dealer A's spread).
options.B: 1.64 = best ask 100.49 (Dealer C) - best bid 98.85 (Dealer A).

rationales.A: Not individual dealer.
rationales.B:
Full statement: 1.64 = best ask 100.49 (Dealer C) - best bid 98.85 (Dealer A); lower than any individual dealer's spread because the dealer with highest bid is not the dealer with lowest ask.

Correct. Per the reading's example: 'The best bid price, 98.85 by Dealer A, is lower than the best ask price, 100.49 by Dealer C. The market spread is thus 100.49 − 98.85 = 1.64, which is lower than any of the dealers' spreads.' Individual spreads: A=1.66, B=1.71, C=1.67. Best bid + best ask = market spread. Midquote = (100.49 + 98.85)/2 = 99.67.
rationales.C: Not correct.

drills.los_text: calculate and interpret effective spreads and VWAP transaction cost estimates

options.A: just the quoted spread.
options.B: always zero.
options.C: for buy: 2 × (Trade price − Midquote at order entry).

rationales.A: Effective differs from quoted.
rationales.B: Real measure.
rationales.C:
Full statement: for buy: 2 × (Trade price − Midquote at order entry); for sell: 2 × (Midquote at order entry − Trade price); estimates the spread that traders would have observed if the quoted ask (buy) or bid (sell) equaled the trade price.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Multiplying this midquote price benchmark transaction cost estimate by 2 produces a statistic called the effective spread. It is the spread that traders would have observed if the quoted ask (for a purchase) or the bid (for a sale) were equal to the trade price. The effective spread is a sensible estimate of transaction costs when orders are filled in single trades.' Formula: Effective spread = 2 × |trade price − midquote at order entry|.

options.A: trade execution prices are better than quoted prices — buy fills below the ask price or sell fills above the bid.
options.B: always worse than quoted.
options.C: quoted prices are illegal.

rationales.A:
Full statement: trade execution prices are better than quoted prices — buy fills below the ask price or sell fills above the bid; effective spread is less than the quoted spread when this happens.

Correct. Per the reading: 'If an order fills at a price better than the quoted price (e.g., a buy order fills at a price below the ask price), the order is said to receive price improvement and the spread is effectively lower. Price improvement occurs when trade execution prices are better than quoted prices. An order that fills at a price outside the quoted spread has an effective spread that is larger than the quoted spread. Such results occur when trade execution prices are worse than quoted prices.'
rationales.B: Opposite.
rationales.C: Not about legality.

questions.stem: The *effective spread* is a *poor estimate of transaction costs* when *most likely*:

options.A: The effective spread is a poor cost estimate when orders always fill immediately in single executions.
options.B: traders split large orders into many parts filled over time — orders often move the market and cause bid/ask prices to rise or fall.
options.C: The effective spread fails only in markets where no orders exist.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Immediate single fills are where the effective spread works BEST.
rationales.B:
Full statement: traders split large orders into many parts filled over time — orders often move the market and cause bid/ask prices to rise or fall; the market impact makes trading expensive (especially for the last parts to fill) but effective spread will not fully identify this cost if computed separately for each trade.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The effective spread is a poor estimate of transaction costs when traders split large orders into many parts to fill over time. Such orders often move the market and cause bid and ask prices to rise or fall. The impact of the order on market prices, called market impact, makes trading expensive — especially for the last parts to fill — but the effective spread will not fully identify this cost if it is computed separately for each trade.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The failure case is real and common: split orders filled over time move quotes, and the effective spread misses that cumulative impact.

questions.curriculum_ref: Effective Spread — poor for split orders

questions.stem: In the Alpha Company example: Trade 1 was a market sell of 2,000 shares at C$10.15 when market was C$10.12 bid / C$10.24 ask. The *effective spread* is *most likely*:

options.A: C$0.12 (quoted spread).
options.C: C$0.06 = 2 × (C$10.18 midpoint − C$10.15 trade price) — less than quoted spread of C$0.12.

rationales.A: Not quoted spread.
rationales.B: Not double quoted.
rationales.C:
Full statement: C$0.06 = 2 × (C$10.18 midpoint − C$10.15 trade price) — less than quoted spread of C$0.12; the trade received price improvement because it sold above the bid.

Correct. Per the reading's example: 'The effective spread for a sell order is 2 × (Midpoint of the market at the time of order entry − Trade price). For the first order, the midpoint of the market at the time of order entry is (C$10.12 + C$10.24)/2 = C$10.18, so that the effective spread is 2 × (C$10.18 − C$10.15) = C$0.06.' Price improvement because effective (C$0.06) < quoted (C$0.12).

questions.stem: *VWAP transaction cost estimate* is calculated as *most likely*:

options.A: for buy: Trade size × (Trade VWAP − VWAP benchmark).
options.B: quoted spread only.
options.C: always zero.

rationales.A:
Full statement: for buy: Trade size × (Trade VWAP − VWAP benchmark); for sell: Trade size × (VWAP benchmark − Trade VWAP); VWAP typically computed as sum of dollar volume of trades divided by total quantity within the interval; often called 'interval VWAP'.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Volume-weighted average price (VWAP) is one of the most widely used benchmark prices that analysts use to estimate transaction costs. Analysts typically compute the VWAP using all trades that occurred from the start of the order until the order was completed, a measure that is often referred to as "interval VWAP." The VWAP is the sum of the total dollar value of the benchmark trades divided by the total quantity of the trades.' Formula: for buys, size × (Trade VWAP − VWAP benchmark); for sells, size × (VWAP benchmark − Trade VWAP).
rationales.B: Different measure.
rationales.C: Real cost measure.

questions.stem: In the Alpha example: Total day 13,000 shares traded (2,000 @ C$10.15 + 3,000 @ C$10.11 + 5,000 @ C$10.01 + 2,000 @ C$10.20 + 1,000 @ C$10.15 = C$131,230). Mutual fund sold 10,000 shares at avg C$10.068 (trade VWAP). The VWAP transaction cost estimate is *most likely*:

options.A: zero.

rationales.A: Real cost.
rationales.B:
Full statement: ~C$266 = 10,000 × (C$10.0946 − C$10.0680); the VWAP benchmark is C$131,230/13,000 = C$10.0946 (all trades that day); trade VWAP is C$10.0680 (only fund's 10,000 shares).

Correct. Per the reading's example: 'The VWAP for the day is the total dollar volume divided by the total number of shares traded. The dollar volume is 2,000 shares × C$10.15 + 3,000 shares × C$10.11 + 5,000 shares × C$10.01 + 2,000 shares × C$10.20 + 1,000 shares at C$10.15 = C$131,230. Dividing this by the 13,000-share total volume gives a VWAP of C$10.0946. A similar calculation using only the sales made by the mutual fund gives a trade VWAP of C$10.0680. The VWAP transaction cost estimate for the sale is the difference multiplied by the 10,000 shares sold: C$266.15 = 10,000 shares × (C$10.0946 − C$10.0680).'
rationales.C: Not that scale.

questions.stem: *VWAP transaction cost estimate interpretation problem* *most likely* is:

options.A: VWAP transaction cost estimates are impossible to interpret under any conditions.
options.B: VWAP cost estimates are always exactly zero by construction.
options.C: when trades being evaluated are substantial fraction of all trades in benchmark, or trades took place at same rate as other trades, Trade VWAP and VWAP benchmark will be nearly equal — suggesting trades were not costly.

rationales.A: Incorrect. They are interpretable — just biased in identifiable situations.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The bias TOWARD zero (when the evaluated trades dominate the benchmark or trade at the same rate as others) is the flaw, and it explains the measure's popularity.
rationales.C:
Full statement: when trades being evaluated are substantial fraction of all trades in benchmark, or trades took place at same rate as other trades, Trade VWAP and VWAP benchmark will be nearly equal — suggesting trades were not costly; but conclusion misleading if trade had substantial price impact; bias toward zero explains why measure is popular.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Interpreting VWAP transaction cost estimates is problematic when the trades being evaluated are a substantial fraction of all trades in the VWAP benchmark, or, more generally, when the trades took place at the same rate as other trades in the market. In both cases, the Trade VWAP and the VWAP benchmark will be nearly equal, which would suggest that the evaluated trades were not costly. But this conclusion would be misleading if the trade had substantial price impact. For example, if a large trader were the only buyer for a given trading period (or interval), the VWAP transaction cost estimate would be zero regardless of the market impact. This bias toward zero helps explain why the measure is so popular. Investment managers like to show their investment sponsors transaction cost estimates that suggest that trading is not expensive.'

questions.stem: *Effective spread relation to quoted spread*: If a large order 'walks down' the limit order book (sell order exceeds bid size), the effective spread will *most likely* be:

options.A: greater than the quoted spread — because the large order size that exceeds the bid size causes the order to walk down the limit order book.
options.B: When a large sell order walks down the book, the effective spread will be less than the quoted spread.
options.C: When a large order exceeds the bid size, the effective spread remains identical to the quoted spread.

rationales.A:
Full statement: greater than the quoted spread — because the large order size that exceeds the bid size causes the order to walk down the limit order book; the average sale price is less than the bid so effective spread is higher than quoted spread.

Correct. Per the reading's example (Alpha Trade 3): 'The effective spread for the third trade is greater than the quoted spread because the large order size, which was greater than the bid size, caused the order to walk down the limit order book. The average sale price was less than the bid so that the effective spread was higher than the quoted spread.' Walking down the book adds market impact beyond the quoted spread.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The direction is the reverse — deeper fills at worse prices WIDEN the effective spread beyond the quote.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Equality holds only when the fill completes at the quote; walking the book breaks it.

drills.los_text: describe the implementation shortfall approach to transaction cost measurement

options.A: Implementation shortfall measures only the commissions paid on executed trades.
options.B: the total cost of implementing an investment decision by capturing all explicit and implicit costs — includes market impact costs, delay costs, and opportunity costs which are often significant for large orders.
options.C: Implementation shortfall measures only the market impact component of trading cost.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Commissions are one slice; IS is COMPREHENSIVE — explicit and implicit costs together.
rationales.B:
Full statement: the total cost of implementing an investment decision by capturing all explicit and implicit costs — includes market impact costs, delay costs, and opportunity costs which are often significant for large orders; views trading from an investment management perspective.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The implementation shortfall method of measuring trading costs addresses the problems associated with the effective spread method. Implementation shortfall is also attractive because it views trading from an investment management perspective and measures the total cost of implementing an investment decision by capturing all explicit and implicit costs. The implementation shortfall method includes the market impact costs and delay costs as well as opportunity costs, which are often significant for large orders.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Impact is captured alongside delay and opportunity costs — the totality of implementation cost.

options.A: Implementation shortfall compares the actual portfolio to its benchmark index.
options.B: Implementation shortfall compares the target portfolio to the planning document.
options.C: the values of the actual portfolio with the paper portfolio (constructed on assumption trades could be arranged at prices that prevailed when the decision to trade was made).

rationales.A: Incorrect. Benchmark-relative return is performance measurement; IS compares actual to the PAPER portfolio.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The comparison is actual results versus the paper portfolio transacted at decision prices.
rationales.C:
Full statement: the values of the actual portfolio with the paper portfolio (constructed on assumption trades could be arranged at prices that prevailed when the decision to trade was made); the prevailing price is the decision price/arrival price/strike price, generally the midquote at the time of decision.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Implementation shortfall compares the values of the actual portfolio with that of a paper portfolio constructed on the assumption that trades could be arranged at the prices that prevailed when the decision to trade is made. The prevailing price — also called the decision price, the arrival price, or the strike price — is generally taken to be the midquote price at the time of the trade decision. The excess of the paper value over the actual value is the implementation shortfall.'

options.A: is the midquote price at the time of the trade decision — the reference point for the paper portfolio.
options.B: is the closing price.
options.C: always the ask price.

rationales.A: Correct. Per the reading: 'The prevailing price — also called the decision price, the arrival price, or the strike price — is generally taken to be the midquote price at the time of the trade decision.' Establishing the reference decision price allows a fair paper-vs-actual comparison.
rationales.B: Not close.
rationales.C: Midquote.

questions.stem: *Implementation shortfall* is *most likely* preferred over effective spread when:

options.A: Implementation shortfall is preferred over effective spread when orders always fill in single executions.
options.B: orders are large and split over time — IS captures market impact costs, delay costs, opportunity costs that arise as prices move away during protracted execution.
options.C: Implementation shortfall is preferred only in markets where no orders exist.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Single fills are the effective spread's home turf.
rationales.B:
Full statement: orders are large and split over time — IS captures market impact costs, delay costs, opportunity costs that arise as prices move away during protracted execution; effective spread misses these when computed per trade.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The implementation shortfall method of measuring trading costs addresses the problems associated with the effective spread method... The implementation shortfall method includes the market impact costs and delay costs as well as opportunity costs, which are often significant for large orders.' Effective spread computed per-trade underestimates market impact of split large orders because it fails to capture cross-trade effects.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The preference case is real: large orders split over time, where IS captures impact, delay, and opportunity costs the spread misses.

questions.stem: The *paper portfolio* in IS *most likely* assumes:

options.A: actual execution prices.
options.B: worst-case prices.
options.C: trades could be arranged at prices prevailing when the decision to trade was made.

rationales.A: Hypothetical prices.
rationales.B: Not worst case.
rationales.C:
Full statement: trades could be arranged at prices prevailing when the decision to trade was made — a hypothetical benchmark measuring what would have been achieved without market impact, delay, or opportunity cost.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Implementation shortfall compares the values of the actual portfolio with that of a paper portfolio constructed on the assumption that trades could be arranged at the prices that prevailed when the decision to trade is made.' The paper portfolio serves as a counterfactual benchmark measuring what could have been achieved with instant, frictionless execution.

questions.stem: *Excess of paper value over actual value* is *most likely*:

options.A: the implementation shortfall — a positive number represents the total costs of implementation.
options.B: always negative.
options.C: the return.

rationales.A:
Full statement: the implementation shortfall — a positive number represents the total costs of implementation; can be decomposed into explicit costs, market impact/spread costs, delay costs, and opportunity costs.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The excess of the paper value over the actual value is the implementation shortfall.' A larger difference means more implementation cost. The measure can be decomposed by components (commission/tax, market impact, delay, opportunity) to identify which parts of the process are most costly.
rationales.B: Typically positive (cost).
rationales.C: Not return.

questions.stem: *Implementation shortfall* addresses which limitation of effective spread *most likely*?

options.A: Implementation shortfall addresses no limitation because the effective spread has none.
options.B: effective spread's failure to capture market impact of split large orders across trades, delay costs, and opportunity costs from unfilled orders.
options.C: Implementation shortfall's advantage is that it is simpler to compute than the effective spread.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The effective spread demonstrably fails on split orders.
rationales.B:
Full statement: effective spread's failure to capture market impact of split large orders across trades, delay costs, and opportunity costs from unfilled orders — IS captures the total drag on portfolio performance not just individual trade slippage.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The implementation shortfall method of measuring trading costs addresses the problems associated with the effective spread method... The implementation shortfall method includes the market impact costs and delay costs as well as opportunity costs, which are often significant for large orders.' Effective spread's per-trade calculation doesn't aggregate cross-trade market impact, whereas IS captures the total decision-to-execution slippage.
rationales.C: Incorrect. IS is MORE complex, not simpler — its advantage is comprehensiveness across impact, delay, and unfilled-order costs.

drills.los_text: describe factors driving the development of electronic trading systems

questions.stem: The *widespread use of electronic trading systems* *most likely* has:

options.A: increased trading costs.
options.B: no impact on costs.
options.C: significantly decreased trading costs for buy-side traders.

rationales.A: Opposite direction.
rationales.B: Substantial impact.
rationales.C:
Full statement: significantly decreased trading costs for buy-side traders — through cost efficiencies from electronic matching systems vs floor-based manual trading systems, forcing dealers and arbitrageurs (main liquidity providers) to pass along benefits via narrower spreads quoted for larger sizes.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The widespread use of electronic trading systems significantly decreased trading costs for buy-side traders. Costs fell as exchanges obtained greater cost efficiencies from using electronic matching systems instead of floor-based, manual trading systems. These technologies also decreased costs and increased efficiencies for the dealers and arbitrageurs, who provide much of the liquidity offered at exchanges. Competition forced them to pass along many of the benefits of their new technologies to buy-side traders in the form of narrower spreads quoted for larger sizes.'

questions.stem: *Advantages of electronic exchange systems* over floor-based systems *most likely* include:

options.A: cheap to operate once built (server rooms vs floors), precisely enforce order precedence and pricing rules without error, keep perfect audit trails, keep hidden orders perfectly hidden (unlike floor brokers), operate continuously around-the-clock, operate when bad weather prevents floor workers.
options.B: Electronic exchange systems offer no advantages over floor-based trading systems, since human floor brokers can negotiate nuances and accommodate order handling that machines cannot replicate.
options.C: Electronic systems offer exactly one advantage over floor-based trading — longer daily trading hours — while matching floors on cost, accuracy, and record keeping.

rationales.A: Correct. Per the reading: 'Compared with floor-based trading systems, electronic order-matching systems enjoy many advantages: Most obviously, electronic systems are cheap to operate once built. Operating in server rooms, they require less physical space than trading floors... Electronic exchange systems do exactly what they are programmed to do. When properly programmed, they precisely enforce the exchange's trading order precedence and pricing rules without error or exception. Electronic exchange systems can also keep perfect audit trails... Electronic exchange systems that support hidden orders keep those orders perfectly hidden. Unlike floor brokers, they never inadvertently or fraudulently reveal their clients' hidden orders to others. In contrast to floor-based brokers and exchange officials, electronic order-matching systems can operate, for the most part, on a continuous, "around-the-clock" basis. Finally, electronic exchanges can operate when bad weather or other events would likely prevent workers from convening on a floor.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The advantages are numerous and decisive — cost, precision, records, speed, scale.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Hours are one item on a longer list: cheap operation, error-free rule enforcement, perfect audit trails.

questions.stem: *Computers* are effective for implementing trading strategies *most likely* because they have:

options.A: Computers implement trading strategies effectively because they possess human intuition.
options.B: infinite attention spans and wide attention scope (watch many instruments simultaneously), extraordinarily fast responses, perfect discipline (do exactly what programmed), and perfect memory retention of any information programmers want to save.
options.C: Computers bring no relevant capabilities to trading strategy implementation.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Intuition is what computers LACK; their edge is attention, speed, and discipline.
rationales.B: Correct. Per the reading: 'Additionally, computers have come to dominate the implementation of many trading strategies because they are so efficient and so unlike human traders: Computers have infinite attention spans and a very wide attention scope. They can continuously watch and respond to information from many instruments and many markets simultaneously and essentially forever. Their responses are extraordinarily fast. Computers are perfectly disciplined and do only what they are instructed (programmed) to do. Computers do not forget any information that their programmers want to save.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Infinite attention spans, simultaneous multi-instrument monitoring, and instant flawless execution are the capabilities.

options.A: Order-driven markets are always dealer-only markets where customers cannot post orders.
options.B: Order-driven markets are always paper-based systems predating electronic trading.
options.C: markets in which orders submitted by traders are arranged based on a rules-based, order-matching system run by an exchange, a broker, or an alternative trading system (ATS).

rationales.A: Incorrect. Dealer-centric quoting is the QUOTE-driven model; order-driven markets match customer orders under rules.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Modern order-driven markets are overwhelmingly electronic — the rules-based matching system defines them, not the medium.
rationales.C:
Full statement: markets in which orders submitted by traders are arranged based on a rules-based, order-matching system run by an exchange, a broker, or an alternative trading system (ATS); organized by most exchanges and electronic communication networks (ECNs).

Correct. Per the reading: 'Electronic trading systems have largely displaced floor-based trading systems in all instruments for which order-driven markets are viable. Order-driven markets — markets in which orders submitted by traders are arranged based on a rules-based, order-matching system run by an exchange, a broker, or an alternative trading system (ATS) — are now organized by most exchanges and electronic communication networks (ECNs).'

questions.stem: The *electronification of bond markets* *most likely* has:

options.A: lagged that of equities, futures, and options markets — despite efforts of many creative developers, most public investors still trade largely over-the-counter with dealers.
options.B: The electronification of bond markets has fully occurred, matching equities in every respect.
options.C: Bond markets have no potential for electronic trading given their structure.

rationales.A:
Full statement: lagged that of equities, futures, and options markets — despite efforts of many creative developers, most public investors still trade largely over-the-counter with dealers; potential for growth is quite large; corporate/municipal bond issues vastly outnumber stock issues.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Much less attention has been given to the market structures of corporate and municipal bond markets, most of which, from the customer's point of view, have changed little since the late 19th century. Despite the efforts of many creative developers of electronic bond trading systems, most public investors in these markets still trade largely over the counter with dealers. The potential for electronic trading systems in these markets — and the attendant growth in electronic trading strategies — is quite large. Such systems undoubtedly will reflect the fact that bond issues — especially municipal bonds — vastly outnumber stock issues.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Bond electronification LAGS — most public investors still trade through dealers.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The potential is substantial; the lag reflects instrument heterogeneity, not impossibility.

questions.stem: The *two co-dependent systems* essential to electronic trading are *most likely*:

options.A: single system.
options.B: traders need high-speed order processing and communication systems to implement electronic trading strategies.
options.C: no systems.

rationales.A: Co-dependent.
rationales.B:
Full statement: traders need high-speed order processing and communication systems to implement electronic trading strategies; exchanges need electronic exchange systems to process vast numbers of orders these traders produce.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The two types of systems are co-dependent: Traders need high-speed order processing and communication systems to implement their electronic trading strategies, and the exchanges need electronic exchange systems to process the vast numbers of orders that these electronic traders produce. The adoption of electronic exchange systems led to huge growth in automated order creation and submission systems.'
rationales.C: Two systems.

questions.stem: *Studies* on electronic trading transaction costs *most likely* show:

options.A: costs unchanged.
options.B: costs increased.
options.C: transaction costs declined with the growth of electronic trading over time.

rationales.A: Declined significantly.
rationales.B: Costs declined.
rationales.C:
Full statement: transaction costs declined with the growth of electronic trading over time; at a given point in time, lower transaction costs are found in markets with greatest intensity of electronic trading (both time-series and cross-sectional results).

Correct. Per the reading: 'Numerous studies show that transaction costs declined with the growth of electronic trading over time. Some studies also show that at a given point in time, lower transaction costs are found in those markets with the greatest intensity of electronic trading. These time-series and cross-sectional results are not surprising. They result from the greater cost efficiencies associated with electronic trading. With the growth of electronic trading, bid–ask spreads decreased substantially. These decreases lowered transaction costs for retail traders and institutions trading small orders.'

options.A: trading the same instrument in multiple venues — venues trading the same instruments have proliferated, and trading in any given instrument now occurs in multiple venues.
options.B: Market fragmentation means all trading in an instrument concentrates on a single exchange.
options.C: Market fragmentation means no trading venues exist for the instrument.

rationales.A:
Full statement: trading the same instrument in multiple venues — venues trading the same instruments have proliferated, and trading in any given instrument now occurs in multiple venues; increases potential for price and liquidity disparities across venues because buyers and sellers often are not in the same venues at the same time.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Markets for many asset classes have become increasingly fragmented throughout the world because venues trading the same instruments have proliferated and trading in any given instrument now occurs in multiple venues. Available liquidity for an instrument on any one exchange now often represents just a small fraction of the aggregate liquidity for that instrument. Market fragmentation — trading the same instrument in multiple venues — increases the potential for price and liquidity disparities across venues because buyers and sellers often are not in the same venues at the same time.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Concentration is the OPPOSITE; fragmentation is the same instrument trading across many venues.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Fragmentation implies venue PROLIFERATION, not absence.

questions.stem: In the US, *exchange-listed equities order flow* is *most likely*:

options.A: single exchange only.
options.B: divided among 11 exchanges, 40 alternative trading systems, and numerous dealers.
options.C: no ATSs exist.

rationales.A: Fragmented.
rationales.B:
Full statement: divided among 11 exchanges, 40 alternative trading systems, and numerous dealers — vs late 20th century when trading mainly occurred on three primary exchanges, a few minor regional exchanges, and in offices of some large institutional broker/dealers.

Correct. Per the reading: 'For example, in the United States, order flow in exchange-listed equities is now divided among 11 exchanges, 40 alternative trading systems, and numerous dealers. In the late 20th century, however, trading mainly occurred on three primary exchanges, a few minor regional exchanges, and in the offices of some large institutional broker/dealers. Alternative trading systems (ATSs), also known as electronic communication networks (ECNs) or multilateral trading facilities (MTFs), are increasingly important trading venues. They function like exchanges but do not exercise regulatory authority over their subscribers except concerning the conduct of their trading in their trading systems.'
rationales.C: 40+ ATSs.

questions.stem: *Alternative trading systems (ATSs)* are also known as *most likely*:

options.A: traditional exchanges.
options.B: brokerages.
options.C: electronic communication networks (ECNs) or multilateral trading facilities (MTFs).

rationales.A: Not traditional.
rationales.B: Trading venues.
rationales.C:
Full statement: electronic communication networks (ECNs) or multilateral trading facilities (MTFs); increasingly important trading venues; function like exchanges but do not exercise regulatory authority over subscribers except concerning trading conduct in their trading systems.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Alternative trading systems (ATSs), also known as electronic communication networks (ECNs) or multilateral trading facilities (MTFs), are increasingly important trading venues. They function like exchanges but do not exercise regulatory authority over their subscribers except concerning the conduct of their trading in their trading systems.'

options.A: create 'super books' that present liquidity across markets for a given instrument.
options.B: reduce liquidity.
options.C: block liquidity.

rationales.A:
Full statement: create 'super books' that present liquidity across markets for a given instrument — offer global views of market depth (available liquidity) for each instrument regardless of which trading venue offers the liquidity.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Electronic algorithmic trading techniques, such as liquidity aggregation and smart order routing, help traders manage the challenges and opportunities presented by fragmentation. Liquidity aggregators create "super books" that present liquidity across markets for a given instrument. These tools offer global views of market depth (available liquidity) for each instrument regardless of which trading venue offers the liquidity.'
rationales.B: Opposite direction.
rationales.C: Facilitate liquidity.

options.A: send orders randomly.
options.B: send orders to the markets that display the best-quoted prices and sizes.
options.C: block routing.

rationales.A: Not random.
rationales.B:
Full statement: send orders to the markets that display the best-quoted prices and sizes; help traders manage market fragmentation by finding best execution across multiple venues.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Smart order-routing algorithms send orders to the markets that display the best-quoted prices and sizes.' For example, the best bid for a Eurodollar future may be on CME and the second best on ELX Markets, a fully electronic futures exchange. Smart order routing directs orders to the best-priced venues.
rationales.C: Best-price routing.

questions.stem: *Traders filling large orders* adapt trading strategies to *most likely*:

options.A: Traders filling large orders adapt by concentrating all activity in a single venue.
options.B: Traders filling large orders adapt by executing everything at once in one block.
options.C: search for liquidity across multiple venues and across time to control market impacts of their trades — response to increasing market fragmentation.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Single-venue concentration ignores the liquidity scattered elsewhere.
rationales.B: Incorrect. All-at-once execution maximizes impact; the adaptation is searching across VENUES and across TIME.
rationales.C:
Full statement: search for liquidity across multiple venues and across time to control market impacts of their trades — response to increasing market fragmentation; algorithms break orders into pieces and submit them randomly across markets over time.

Correct. Per the reading: 'With increasing market fragmentation, traders filling large orders now adapt their trading strategies to search for liquidity across multiple venues and across time to control the market impacts of their trades. Electronic algorithmic trading techniques, such as liquidity aggregation and smart order routing, help traders manage the challenges and opportunities presented by fragmentation.'

questions.stem: *Available liquidity for an instrument* on any single exchange *most likely*:

options.A: often represents just a small fraction of the aggregate liquidity for that instrument — due to fragmentation across multiple venues.
options.B: Any single exchange's displayed liquidity represents all of the aggregate liquidity for the instrument.
options.C: A single exchange's displayed liquidity represents nothing about the instrument's tradability.

rationales.A:
Full statement: often represents just a small fraction of the aggregate liquidity for that instrument — due to fragmentation across multiple venues; drives need for aggregation tools and smart routing.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Available liquidity for an instrument on any one exchange now often represents just a small fraction of the aggregate liquidity for that instrument.' This creates the challenge that motivates development of liquidity aggregation and smart order routing.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Fragmentation means each venue holds a SLICE, often a small one.
rationales.C: Incorrect. It represents real but partial liquidity — a fraction of the cross-venue aggregate.

drills.los_text: identify and contrast the types of electronic traders

options.A: High-frequency traders typically hold positions for months, harvesting slow-moving premia.
options.B: generally complete round trips composed of a purchase followed by a sale (or sale then purchase) within a minute and often as quickly as a few milliseconds.
options.C: High-frequency traders trade only once a year at index reconstitution.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Month-long holding is conventional investing; HFT round trips complete within a MINUTE, often milliseconds.
rationales.B:
Full statement: generally complete round trips composed of a purchase followed by a sale (or sale then purchase) within a minute and often as quickly as a few milliseconds; may trade in and out of an actively traded security more than a thousand times per day but usually only in small sizes.

Correct. Per the reading: 'High-frequency traders (HFTs) generally complete round trips composed of a purchase followed by a sale (or a sale followed by a purchase) within a minute and often as quickly as a few milliseconds. During a day, they may trade in and out of an actively traded security or contract more than a thousand times — but usually only in small sizes.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. HFT activity is continuous and extremely short-horizon.

options.A: Low-latency traders are exclusively high-frequency market-making firms.
options.B: No category of low-latency traders exists in modern markets.
options.C: news traders (trade on electronic news feeds) and certain parasitic traders (speculators who base predictions on information about orders others intend to fill).

rationales.A: Incorrect. HFTs are a subset; NEWS traders and certain parasitic traders also compete on latency.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The category is defined and populated — speed-dependent strategies beyond pure HFT.
rationales.C:
Full statement: news traders (trade on electronic news feeds) and certain parasitic traders (speculators who base predictions on information about orders others intend to fill); parasitic traders include front runners (trade in front of liquidity demanders) and quote matchers (trade in front of liquidity suppliers); may hold positions for as long as a day or longer, unlike HFTs.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Low-latency traders include news traders who trade on electronic news feeds and certain parasitic traders. Parasitic traders are speculators who base their predictions about future prices on information they obtain about orders that other traders intend, or will soon intend, to fill. Parasitic traders include front runners, who trade in front of traders who demand liquidity, and quote matchers, who trade in front of traders who supply liquidity. When trying to open or close positions, low-latency traders often need to send or cancel orders very quickly in response to new information. In contrast to HFTs, low-latency traders may hold their positions for as long as a day and sometimes longer.'

options.A: subscribe to high-speed electronic news feeds, quickly analyze releases to determine if information will move markets and in which direction, send marketable orders (instructions to fill at best available price).
options.B: Electronic news traders trade only random orders unrelated to information flow.
options.C: Electronic news traders deliberately avoid news to reduce information risk.

rationales.A:
Full statement: subscribe to high-speed electronic news feeds, quickly analyze releases to determine if information will move markets and in which direction, send marketable orders (instructions to fill at best available price); profit when they can execute against 'stale orders' — orders that don't yet reflect the new information.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Electronic news traders subscribe to high-speed electronic news feeds that report news releases made by corporations, governments, and other aggregators of information. They then quickly analyze these releases to determine whether the information they contain will move the markets and, if so, in which direction. They trade on this information by sending marketable orders — instructions to fill the order at the best available price — to wherever they expect they may be filled. News traders profit when they can execute against stale orders — orders that do not yet reflect the new information.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Their strategy is entirely news-DRIVEN — machine-readable feeds parsed in microseconds.
rationales.C: Incorrect. News is the input: high-speed feeds analyzed instantly for direction and materiality.

options.A: trade all sizes.
options.B: make markets by placing bids and offers expecting to profit from round trips at favorable net spreads.
options.C: hold huge positions.

rationales.A: Wary market makers.
rationales.B:
Full statement: make markets by placing bids and offers expecting to profit from round trips at favorable net spreads; those at highest frequencies are very wary — on first indication prices may move against inventory positions, immediately take liquidity on opposite side to reduce exposure; won't hold large inventory in actively traded stocks; cancel orders before scheduled news releases.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Electronic dealers, like all dealers, make markets by placing bids (prices at which they are willing to buy) and offers (prices at which they are willing to sell) with the expectation that they can profit from round trips at favorable net spreads. Those who trade at the highest frequencies tend to be very wary. On the first indication that prices may move against their inventory positions... they immediately take liquidity by executing on the opposite side to reduce their exposure. They generally will not hold large inventory positions in actively traded stocks... Electronic dealers, like all other dealers, also keep track of scheduled news releases. They cancel their orders just before releases to avoid offering liquidity to traders who can act faster than they can.'
rationales.C: Wary.

options.A: Electronic arbitrageurs trade a single instrument in a single market, avoiding cross-market activity.
options.B: No arbitrage opportunities exist in electronic markets, so no such traders operate.
options.C: look across markets for arbitrage opportunities in which they can buy an undervalued instrument and sell a similar overvalued one.

rationales.A: Incorrect. CROSS-market comparison is the strategy's essence.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Opportunities appear continuously at small scale — buying the undervalued instrument against selling a similar overvalued one.
rationales.C:
Full statement: look across markets for arbitrage opportunities in which they can buy an undervalued instrument and sell a similar overvalued one; combination is called an arbitrage portfolio; positions are called legs; try to construct arbitrage portfolios at minimum cost and risk.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Electronic arbitrageurs look across markets for arbitrage opportunities in which they can buy an undervalued instrument and sell a similar overvalued one. The combination of these two positions is called an arbitrage portfolio, and the positions are called legs. Electronic arbitrageurs try to construct their arbitrage portfolios at minimum cost and risk.' Three strategies: take liquidity on both sides, offer liquidity on one side, offer liquidity on both sides.

options.A: use artificial intelligence to identify when large traders (or many small traders) are trying to fill orders on the same side of the market.
options.B: Electronic front runners trade only after all other market participants have completed their orders.
options.C: No category of electronic front running exists in modern markets.

rationales.A:
Full statement: use artificial intelligence to identify when large traders (or many small traders) are trying to fill orders on the same side of the market; purchase when they believe imbalance will push market up, sell when opposite; search for predictable patterns in order submissions, trades, or relations between trades and other events.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Electronic front runners are low-latency traders who use artificial intelligence methods to identify when large traders, or many small traders, are trying to fill orders on the same side of the market. They will purchase when they believe that an imbalance of buy orders over sell orders will push the market up and sell when they believe the opposite. Their order anticipation strategies try to identify predictable patterns in order submission. They may search for patterns in order submissions, trades, or the relations between trades and other events.' Dealers and brokers cannot legally front run orders their own clients have submitted, but they can trade on publicly observable patterns.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Trading AFTER everyone is the opposite of the strategy; they trade AHEAD of detected order flow.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The practice is recognized — AI-driven detection of large or correlated same-side order flow, traded in front of.

options.A: Electronic quote matchers trade in front of liquidity demanders, anticipating market orders.
options.B: trade in front of traders who *supply* liquidity — try to exploit option values of standing orders (limit orders waiting to be filled).
options.C: Electronic quote matchers operate with no defined strategy.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Front running DEMANDERS is the front runner's game; quote matchers exploit liquidity SUPPLIERS.
rationales.B:
Full statement: trade in front of traders who *supply* liquidity — try to exploit option values of standing orders (limit orders waiting to be filled); buy when they believe they can rely on standing buy orders to get out of positions, sell when same for sell orders; take positions with potentially limited losses.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Electronic quote matchers try to exploit the option values of standing orders. Standing orders are limit orders waiting to be filled. Options to trade are valuable to quote matchers because they allow them to take positions with potentially limited losses. Quote matchers buy when they believe they can rely on standing buy orders to get out of their positions, and they sell when they can do the same with standing sell orders.' Parasitic traders include front runners (trade in front of liquidity demanders) and quote matchers (trade in front of liquidity suppliers).
rationales.C: Incorrect. The strategy is defined: exploit the option value of standing limit orders waiting to be filled.

questions.stem: *Buy-side traders and their brokers*, aware of front runners, *most likely* respond by:

options.A: Buy-side traders aware of front runners welcome detection, since attention improves their fills.
options.B: Buy-side traders respond to front runners by trading in regular, predictable patterns.
options.C: randomizing their strategies to make them more difficult to detect — submitting orders at random times instead of regular intervals, submitting various sizes instead of same size, hiding among other orders.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Detection is the threat — it invites being traded against.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Predictability aids the predator; the response is RANDOMIZING order timing and sizing.
rationales.C:
Full statement: randomizing their strategies to make them more difficult to detect — submitting orders at random times instead of regular intervals, submitting various sizes instead of same size, hiding among other orders; but hiding large liquidity-demanding trades is always challenging.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Buy-side traders, and the brokers who provide them with algorithms to manage large orders, are aware of the efforts that electronic traders make to detect and front run their orders. Accordingly, they randomize their strategies to make them more difficult to detect. They submit orders at random times instead of at regular intervals, and they submit various sizes instead of the same size. Although these techniques make detection more difficult, hiding large, liquidity-demanding trades is always challenging because sophisticated traders can ultimately identify them by the inevitable relation between prices and volumes that they create.'

drills.los_text: describe characteristics and uses of electronic trading systems

options.A: are programmed strategies using combinations or sequences of simple orders, advanced orders, or multiple orders to achieve objectives.
options.B: Trading algorithms process only one simple order at a time.
options.C: Trading algorithms submit market orders exclusively.

rationales.A:
Full statement: are programmed strategies using combinations or sequences of simple orders, advanced orders, or multiple orders to achieve objectives; extensively used by buy-side traders to trade small orders and reduce price impacts of large trades; break large orders and submit pieces to various markets over time; submit orders at random times/sizes/exchanges to hide origins.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Algorithms ("algos" for short) are programmed strategies for filling orders. Algorithms may use combinations or sequences of simple orders, advanced orders, or multiple orders to achieve their objectives. Buy-side traders use algorithms, often provided by brokers, extensively to trade small orders and to reduce the price impacts of large trades. For example, many algorithms break up large orders and submit the pieces to various markets over time. Breaking up orders makes it difficult for other traders to infer that a trader is trying to fill a large order. The algorithms typically submit the orders at random times, in random sizes, and sometimes to randomly selected exchanges to hide their common origin.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Algos orchestrate COMBINATIONS and sequences of orders toward an objective.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Limit orders, advanced order types, and multi-venue routing are all in the toolkit.

options.A: VWAP algorithms concentrate all trading at the market open.
options.B: attempt to obtain volume-weighted average fill price close to (or better than) VWAP of all trades arranged within a prespecified time interval.
options.C: VWAP algorithms concentrate all trading at the market close.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Open-only execution ignores the day's volume distribution the algo is built to track.
rationales.B:
Full statement: attempt to obtain volume-weighted average fill price close to (or better than) VWAP of all trades arranged within a prespecified time interval; participate in equal fraction of trading volume throughout the interval; forecast volumes based on historical volume profile and current volumes; trade more during high-volume periods (open/close), less during low-volume.

Correct. Per the reading: 'VWAP algorithms attempt to obtain a volume-weighted average fill price that is close to (or better than) the volume-weighted average price (VWAP) of all trades arranged within a prespecified time interval. To minimize the variation between the actual average fill price and the VWAP over the interval, these algorithms try to participate in an equal fraction of all trading volume throughout the interval. To do so, they forecast volumes based on the historical volume profile and on current volumes. The algorithm trades more during periods of historically high volume (e.g., around market open and close) and when the market has been more active than normal. It trades less during periods of relatively low volume.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The algo spreads execution across the horizon in proportion to expected volume, targeting the interval VWAP or better.

options.A: wait forever.
options.B: never cancel.
options.C: cancel immediately if they do not find liquidity.

rationales.A: Cancel if no fill.
rationales.B: IOC cancels.
rationales.C:
Full statement: cancel immediately if they do not find liquidity; used to discover hidden orders that may stand in the spread; because they cancel immediately, are also hidden and don't reveal trade intentions; common orders in electronic markets.

Correct. Per the reading: 'A common type of order by far is the immediate or cancel (IOC) limit order. Traders use these orders to discover hidden orders that may stand in the spread between a market's quoted bid and ask prices. Because they cancel immediately if they do not find liquidity, these orders are also hidden and thus do not reveal trade intentions.'

options.A: submitting a small IOC limit order for only a few shares at a price where hidden orders may exist.
options.B: large order execution.
options.C: no such practice.

rationales.A:
Full statement: submitting a small IOC limit order for only a few shares at a price where hidden orders may exist; if pinging order trades, they know hidden order is present at that price (but not full size); allows adjustment of trading strategies based on discovered liquidity.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Some electronic traders try to discover hidden orders by pinging the market: They submit a small IOC limit order for only a few shares at the price at which they are looking for hidden orders. If the pinging order trades, they know that a hidden order is present at that price; however, they do not know the full size of the order (which they can discover only by trading with it). Traders then may use this information to adjust their trading strategies.'
rationales.B: Small IOC probe.
rationales.C: Common practice.

options.A: permanent orders.
options.B: exposed limit orders that electronic traders submit and then cancel shortly thereafter, often within a second.
options.C: no such orders.

rationales.A: Very short-lived.
rationales.B:
Full statement: exposed limit orders that electronic traders submit and then cancel shortly thereafter, often within a second; submitted by electronic dealers and algorithmic buy-side traders when they don't want their orders to stand in the market — but want other traders to see they're willing to trade at displayed price.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Electronic markets often have flickering quotes, which are exposed limit orders that electronic traders submit and then cancel shortly thereafter, often within a second. Electronic dealers and algorithmic buy-side traders submit and repeatedly cancel and resubmit their orders when they do not want their orders to stand in the market; rather, they want other traders to see that they are willing to trade at the displayed price. Traders who wish to trade with a flickering quote can place a hidden limit order at the price where the quote is flickering. If the flickering order returns, it will hit their hidden limit order.'
rationales.C: Common practice.

options.A: spreads are narrow.
options.B: spread is exactly zero.
options.C: bid-ask spreads are wide — dealers often willing to trade at better prices than quoted.

rationales.A: Wide spreads.
rationales.B: Wide-spread phenomenon.
rationales.C:
Full statement: bid-ask spreads are wide — dealers often willing to trade at better prices than quoted; when another trader quotes better price, dealer instantly quotes even better price; game of leapfrog may ensue as dealer jumps ahead again.

Correct. Per the reading: 'When bid–ask spreads are wide, dealers often are willing to trade at better prices than they quote. They quote wide spreads because they hope to trade at more favorable prices. When another trader quotes a better price, dealers often immediately quote an even better price. For example, if the market is 20 bid, offered at 28, and a buy-side trader bids at 21, a dealer might instantly bid at 22... This behavior frustrates buy-side traders, who then must quote a better price to maintain order precedence. If the spread is sufficiently wide, a game of leapfrog may ensue as the dealer jumps ahead again.'

questions.stem: *Machine learning* in electronic trading is *most likely* effective when:

options.A: problems repeat regularly and often — stable processes generate vast amounts of data (as in active financial markets).
options.B: volatility is extreme.
options.C: novel problems only.

rationales.A:
Full statement: problems repeat regularly and often — stable processes generate vast amounts of data (as in active financial markets); often useless or worse when trading becomes extraordinary (volatilities shoot up) because these episodes have few precedents; many traders shut down when volatility spikes.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Machine learning, also known as data mining, uses advanced statistical methods to characterize data structures... Machine-learning methods produce models based on observed empirical regularities rather than on theoretical principles identified by analysts. These methods can be powerful when stable processes generate vast amounts of data, such as occurs in active financial markets. Many trading problems are ideally suited for machine-learning analyses because the problems repeat regularly and often. For such problems, machine-based learning systems can be extraordinarily powerful. However, these systems are often useless — or worse — when trading becomes extraordinary (e.g., when volatilities shoot up). Machine-learning systems frequently do not produce useful information during volatility episodes because these episodes have few precedents from which the machines can learn.'
rationales.B: Poor for extremes.
rationales.C: Effective in stable periods.

drills.los_text: describe comparative advantages of low-latency traders

questions.stem: *Reacting to an event* electronically involves three major steps *most likely*:

options.A: one step only.
options.B: (1) trader must learn that event took place.
options.C: no steps.

rationales.A: Three-step process.
rationales.B:
Full statement: (1) trader must learn that event took place; (2) trader must respond to new information with new order instruction; (3) trader must send, and exchange must receive, the new instruction — steps 1 and 3 minimized via fast communications; step 2 via fast computers.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Reacting to an event consists of three major steps, each of which involves many smaller steps beyond the scope of this discussion: 1. The trader must learn that the event took place. 2. The trader must respond to the new information with a new order instruction. 3. The trader must send, and the exchange must receive, the new instruction. Traders must use very fast communication systems to minimize the latencies associated with steps 1 and 3 (communicating in and out), and they must use very fast computer systems to minimize the latency associated with step 2 (responding).'
rationales.C: Three steps.

options.A: Collocation is banned by securities regulators in major markets.
options.B: Collocation is not a practice that exists in electronic markets.
options.C: the practice where exchanges allow electronic traders to place their servers in the rooms where the exchange servers operate.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Collocation is legal and commercially offered by exchanges.
rationales.B: Incorrect. It is standard: traders' servers placed in the exchange's own data center to minimize transmission latency.
rationales.C:
Full statement: the practice where exchanges allow electronic traders to place their servers in the rooms where the exchange servers operate; exchanges charge substantial fees for collocation space and related services; concerns about fairness dictate communication lines connecting proprietary servers to exchange servers be of same length for all customers buying same class of collocation service.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Many exchanges allow electronic traders to place their servers in the rooms where the exchange servers operate, a practice called collocation. Exchanges charge substantial fees for collocation space and related services, such as air conditioning and power. Note that even within collocation centers, concerns about fairness dictate that the communication lines connecting proprietary servers to exchange servers all be of the same length for all customers buying the same class of collocation service.'

options.A: no message can travel faster than the speed of light (300,000 km/s in vacuum, ~31% slower in optical fiber).
options.B: no physics limits.
options.C: distance doesn't matter.

rationales.A:
Full statement: no message can travel faster than the speed of light (300,000 km/s in vacuum, ~31% slower in optical fiber); great circle distance Chicago-NY is 1,146 km (~8 ms round trip); NY-London is 5,576 km (~37 ms round trip); no trader at any significant distance from information source or trading venue can effectively compete with traders who have minimized combined distances.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Electronic traders and brokers locate their computers as close as possible to the exchanges at which they trade to minimize latencies resulting from physics: No message can travel faster than the speed of light. At 300,000 kilometers (186,000 miles) per second in a vacuum, light travels 300 kilometers in a millisecond... Communication latencies are particularly important when messages must travel significant distances. For example, the great circle (shortest) distances between Chicago and New York and between New York and London are, respectively, 1,146 kilometers and 5,576 kilometers. Thus, round-trip communications between these two pairs of cities have minimum latencies of approximately 8 and 37 milliseconds simply because of the speed of light. (The actual minimum latencies are longer because the speed of light in standard optical fiber is 31% slower than the speed of light in a vacuum.)'
rationales.B: Real physical limit.
rationales.C: Distance is fundamental.

questions.stem: *Line-of-sight microwave channels* *most likely* are preferred to fiber-optic and copper because:

options.A: Line-of-sight microwave channels offer no speed advantage over fiber-optic cable.
options.B: microwaves travel through air at just slightly below speed of light, whereas signals travel through fiber-optic channels and copper wires only two-thirds as quickly.
options.C: Microwave transmission is slower than fiber-optic transmission, but cheaper to operate.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The speed difference is the entire rationale.
rationales.B:
Full statement: microwaves travel through air at just slightly below speed of light, whereas signals travel through fiber-optic channels and copper wires only two-thirds as quickly; also minimize number of electronic routers and switches through which communications pass (each adds latency).

Correct. Per the reading: 'Electronic traders and brokers also use the fastest communication technologies they can obtain to collect and transmit information when any distance separates the places where information events occur from the places where they act on those events. To that end, they use the fastest and most direct communication lines that are available. For example, they prefer line-of-sight microwave channels to fiber-optic and copper channels because of the differences in speed of electromagnetic wave propagation through these materials. (Microwaves travel through air at just slightly below the speed of light, whereas signals travel through fiber-optic channels and copper wires only two-thirds as quickly.) They also ensure that their communications pass through the fewest electronic routers and switches possible because passage through each of these devices adds its latency to the total latency of the line.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Microwaves through AIR travel just under lightspeed; signals in fiber and copper propagate meaningfully slower.

questions.stem: *Fast computations* strategies for electronic traders *most likely* include:

options.A: Electronic traders pursuing fast computations rely on deliberately slowed, underclocked processors, accepting lower speeds in exchange for the thermal stability that continuous trading requires.
options.B: Fast computation for electronic trading requires no hardware or software optimization, since exchange matching engines impose uniform processing delays that equalize all participants.
options.C: very fast overclocked processors (with liquid cooling), fast memory (avoiding physical disk drive latencies), specialized processors (sometimes on gallium arsenide vs silicon), simple efficient operating systems (variants of MS-DOS due to simplicity), assembler language for repeated functions, avoiding interpreter languages like Python for time-critical code.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The direction is the opposite — OVERCLOCKED processors with liquid cooling.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Optimization is comprehensive: fast memory avoiding disk latency, specialized processors, streamlined code.
rationales.C: Correct. Per the reading: 'First and most obviously, they use very fast computers. They overclock their processors (i.e., run them faster than the processor designers intended) and use liquid cooling systems to keep them from melting. They store all information in fast memory to avoid the latencies associated with physical disk drives... They sometimes use specialized processors designed to solve their specific trading problems quickly, and they may even use processors etched on gallium arsenide rather than silicon. Electronic traders also must run very efficient software. They often use simple and specialized operating systems to avoid the overhead associated with supporting operating system functions they do not use. Remarkably, many electronic trading systems run under variants of the original MS-DOS operating system because of its simplicity. Electronic traders optimize their computer code for speed. They often write important functions that they repeatedly use in assembler language to ensure that they run quickly.'

options.A: contain prearranged action plans — traders reduce their decision latencies by doing analyses before events instead of afterward.
options.B: Contingency tables waste electronic traders' time by requiring analysis of events that may never occur.
options.C: Contingency tables serve no purpose in latency-sensitive trading.

rationales.A:
Full statement: contain prearranged action plans — traders reduce their decision latencies by doing analyses before events instead of afterward; seeing an event (e.g., bid increase), they respond by simply looking up optimal response in a contingency table stored in memory; must be kept up to date and include responses for most-likely events.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Some electronic traders also reduce latency by creating contingency tables that contain prearranged action plans. For example, suppose that a bid rises in a market in which electronic traders are active. In response to the increased bid, traders may want to raise their bids or offers. The decision to do so may depend on their inventory positions and perhaps on many other factors as well. To decide what to do following an increased bid may require substantial analyses, which take time. Traders can reduce their decision latencies by doing these analyses before the bid increases instead of afterward. Seeing the increased bid, they can respond by simply looking up the optimal response in a contingency table stored in memory. To be most useful, the contingency tables must be kept up to date and must include responses for most-likely events.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Pre-computation is the SAVINGS — analysis done before events instead of after.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The purpose is decision-latency reduction: prearranged action plans triggered instantly.

questions.stem: The *ideal use of Python* by electronic traders *most likely* is:

options.A: always Python for speed.
options.B: when speed of coding is more important than speed of execution — for ever-changing sets of conditions or exceptions.
options.C: never Python.

rationales.A: Python not for pure speed.
rationales.B:
Full statement: when speed of coding is more important than speed of execution — for ever-changing sets of conditions or exceptions; traders can code faster and more accurately in high-level languages; may later recode routines in lower-level languages to make them run faster.

Correct. Per the reading: 'And they avoid using such languages as Python because they are interpreter languages that compile (create executable machine code) as they run, rather than compiling only once when first written. Some electronic trading problems change so frequently that speed of coding is more important than speed of execution. For example, some problems depend on ever-changing sets of conditions or exceptions that present or constrain profit opportunities. For such problems, traders use high-level languages (e.g., Python), because they can code faster and more accurately in these languages than in lower-level languages, such as C++. If they expect that the software will remain useful, they may later recode their routines in other languages to make them run faster.'
rationales.C: Python has role.
