drills.los_text: describe the risks associated with electronic trading and how regulators mitigate them

options.A: no barriers.
options.B: unlimited competition.
options.C: expensive state-of-the-art high-frequency trading technologies as barriers to entry that can create natural monopolies.

rationales.A: Real barriers.
rationales.B: Concentration.
rationales.C:
Full statement: expensive state-of-the-art high-frequency trading technologies as barriers to entry that can create natural monopolies; benefits from electronic trading may erode if only a few HFTs survive and can exploit their unique positions; many HFTs quitting because they cannot compete effectively.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The competition among high-frequency traders (HFTs) has created an "arms race" in which each trader tries to be faster than the next. Consequently, the state-of-the-art, high-frequency trading technologies necessary to compete successfully are now very expensive, making entry quite costly. These costs form barriers to entry that can create natural monopolies. Although substantial evidence suggests that electronic trading benefits the markets, these benefits may erode if only a few HFTs survive and can exploit their unique positions. Already, many HFTs are quitting the markets because they cannot compete effectively.'

options.A: arise when failure hurts more than just the entity responsible.
options.B: no such risks.
options.C: always compensated.

rationales.A:
Full statement: arise when failure hurts more than just the entity responsible; particularly problematic when responsible entity is not required or unable to compensate others for costs its failure imposes; caused by electronic exchange trading system failures or excessive orders submitted by electronic traders.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Electronic trading created new systemic risks that concern regulators and practitioners. A systemic risk is a risk that some failure will hurt more than just the entity responsible for the failure. Systemic risks are particularly problematic when the responsible entity is not required or is unable to compensate others for the costs its failure imposes on them. When people do not bear the full costs of their behaviors, they tend not to be as careful in avoiding damaging behaviors as they otherwise would be. Systemic risks associated with fast trading may be caused by electronic exchange trading system failures or excessive orders submitted by electronic traders.'
rationales.B: Real risks.
rationales.C: Uncompensated.

questions.stem: The *Knight Capital trading failure* on 1 August 2012 *most likely*:

options.A: The Knight Capital failure of 1 August 2012 was a minor incident with limited financial consequence.
options.B: was a runaway algorithm — due to a software programming mistake, Knight sent millions of orders to markets over 45-minute period when it intended only 212 orders.
options.C: The Knight Capital failure was caused by a human trader's manual order entry error.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The event was near-fatal to the firm — hundreds of millions lost in under an hour.
rationales.B:
Full statement: was a runaway algorithm — due to a software programming mistake, Knight sent millions of orders to markets over 45-minute period when it intended only 212 orders; produced 4 million executions involving 397 stocks; Knight lost $400 million.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Runaway algorithms produce streams of unintended orders that result from programming mistakes. The problems sometimes occur when programmers do not anticipate some contingency. The Knight Capital trading failure on 1 August 2012 may be the most extreme example of a runaway algorithm incident. Owing to a software programming mistake, Knight sent millions of orders to the markets over a 45-minute period when it intended only to fill 212 orders, some of which normally might have been broken up but none of which would have generated so many orders. These orders produced 4 million executions involving 397 stocks. Knight lost $400 million in the incident.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The cause was SOFTWARE — a programming mistake sent millions of unintended orders over 45 minutes.

options.A: The 6 May 2010 Flash Crash was not tied to any single identifiable order.
options.B: The Flash Crash was a slow, orderly decline spread evenly across the session.
options.C: was triggered by a large institutional trader (Waddell & Reed) selling $4.1B in E-mini S&P 500 futures using an algorithm designed to participate in fixed fraction of market volume.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Investigation traced a specific trigger — a $4.1B E-mini S&P 500 futures sale executed by algorithm.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The crash was FAST — a plunge and recovery within minutes, not a slow drift.
rationales.C:
Full statement: was triggered by a large institutional trader (Waddell & Reed) selling $4.1B in E-mini S&P 500 futures using an algorithm designed to participate in fixed fraction of market volume; initial trades depressed S&P 500 futures prices, trading volumes increased as arbitrageurs traded, causing algorithm to increase order submissions, exacerbating problem; market recovered after CME briefly halted E-mini futures trading; E-mini dropped ~5% in 5 minutes then recovered nearly fully.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Overlarge orders demand more liquidity than the market can provide. In these events, a trader — often inexperienced — will try to execute a marketable order that is too large for the market to handle without severely disrupting prices in the time given to fill the order. The 6 May 2010 Flash Crash occurred as a result of such an order. The crash was triggered when a large institutional trader tried to sell $4.1 billion in E-mini S&P 500 futures contracts using an algorithm over a short period. The algorithm was designed to participate in a fixed fraction of the market volume. When the initial trades depressed S&P 500 futures prices, trading volumes increased substantially as arbitrageurs and others started to trade. The increase in trading volumes caused the algorithm to increase the rate of its order submissions, which exacerbated the problem. The market reverted to its former levels after the Chicago Mercantile Exchange briefly halted trading in the E-mini S&P 500 futures contract, and the large order eventually was filled.'

options.A: occur when a manual trader submits a larger order than intended (hits wrong key or key more often than intended).
options.B: unique to electronic.
options.C: no impact.

rationales.A:
Full statement: occur when a manual trader submits a larger order than intended (hits wrong key or key more often than intended); not unique to electronic trading systems, but consequences often greater in electronic systems because of speed at which they operate and because clerks often catch these errors in manual trading systems before problems.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Fat finger errors occur when a manual trader submits a larger order than intended. They are called fat finger errors because they sometimes occur when a trader hits the wrong key or hits a key more often than intended. These types of errors are not unique to electronic trading systems, but their consequences are often greater in electronic systems because of the speed at which they operate and because clerks often catch these errors in manual trading systems before they cause problems.'
rationales.B: Not unique.
rationales.C: Greater impact in electronic.

options.A: no such threat.
options.B: are created deliberately to disrupt markets.
options.C: always accidental.

rationales.A: Real threat.
rationales.B:
Full statement: are created deliberately to disrupt markets; perpetrators may be market manipulators, aggrieved employees (traders or software engineers), or terrorists; traders conducting denial-of-service attacks designed to overwhelm competitors' electronic trading systems with excessive quotes also create malevolent order streams.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Malevolent order streams are created deliberately to disrupt the markets. The perpetrators may be market manipulators; aggrieved employees, such as traders or software engineers; or terrorists. Traders conducting denial-of-service attacks designed to overwhelm their competitors' electronic trading systems with excessive quotes also may create malevolent order streams.'
rationales.C: Deliberate.

questions.stem: *Solutions to systemic risk problems* *most likely* are multifold and include:

options.A: one solution.
options.B: no solutions.
options.C: test software thoroughly before use (exchanges conduct mock trading sessions).

rationales.A: Multiple solutions.
rationales.B: Multiple solutions.
rationales.C:
Full statement: test software thoroughly before use (exchanges conduct mock trading sessions); rigorous market access controls so only approved orders enter; rigorous access controls on software developers (code must be read/vouched by at least one developer besides author); real-time surveillance of order flow to ensure conformance to preset parameters; brokers must surveil all client orders and cannot allow sponsored naked access; some exchanges have adopted price limits and trade halts.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The solutions to the systemic risk problems associated with electronic trading systems are multifold: Most obviously, traders must test software thoroughly before using it in live trading. Exchanges often conduct mock trading sessions to allow developers to test their software. Rigorous market access controls must ensure that only those orders coming from approved sources enter electronic order-matching systems. Rigorous access controls on software developers must ensure that only authorized developers can change software. Best practice mandates that these controls also include the requirement that all software be read, understood, and vouched for by at least one developer besides its author. The electronic traders who generate orders and the electronic exchanges that receive orders must surveil their order flow in real time... Brokers must surveil all client orders... Brokers must not allow their clients to enter orders directly into exchange trading systems — a process called sponsored naked access... Some exchanges have adopted price limits and trade halts to stop trading when prices move too quickly.'

options.A: stop trading when prices move too quickly — rules stop trading when excess demands for liquidity occur.
options.B: always harmful.
options.C: no such rules.

rationales.A:
Full statement: stop trading when prices move too quickly — rules stop trading when excess demands for liquidity occur; prevent extreme price changes that can occur when market orders arrive and no liquidity is present; most brokers now automatically convert market orders into marketable limit orders to ensure they don't trade at unreasonable prices.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Some exchanges have adopted price limits and trade halts to stop trading when prices move too quickly. These rules stop trading when excess demands for liquidity occur. They also prevent the extreme price changes that can occur in electronic markets when market orders arrive and no liquidity is present. Most brokers now automatically convert market orders into marketable limit orders to ensure that they do not trade at unreasonable prices.'
rationales.B: Have protective role.
rationales.C: Common practice.

drills.los_text: describe abusive trading practices that real-time surveillance of markets may detect

options.A: prevent all trading.
options.B: detect potential market abuse while it is happening.
options.C: no surveillance.

rationales.A: Enable trading.
rationales.B:
Full statement: detect potential market abuse while it is happening — including front running, market manipulation (bluffing, gunning, squeezing, cornering), trading for market impact, rumormongering, wash trading, and spoofing — allowing rapid intervention.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Regulators around the world recognize that real-time market monitoring and surveillance systems allow faster responses to potential crises and market abuses with the potential for rapid intervention to prevent or minimize damages... The goal of real-time market surveillance is to detect potential market abuse while it is happening. Real-time surveillance often can detect the following damaging behaviors: Front running; Market manipulation.' Market manipulation strategies include bluffing, squeezing, cornering, and gunning.
rationales.C: Detects abuse.

options.A: is always legal.
options.B: no such practice.
options.C: involves buying in front of anticipated purchases and selling in front of anticipated sales.

rationales.A: Depends on info source.
rationales.B: Legal or illegal.
rationales.C:
Full statement: involves buying in front of anticipated purchases and selling in front of anticipated sales; illegal if front runners acquire information about orders improperly (e.g., a tip from a broker handling a large order); legal if based on properly obtained information (e.g., watching a market data feed); increases transaction costs for the front-run traders.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Front running involves buying in front of anticipated purchases and selling in front of anticipated sales. In most jurisdictions, front running is illegal if the front runners acquire their information about orders improperly — for example, by a tip from a broker handling a large order. Some traders use electronic artificial intelligence systems to identify when traders are filling large orders over time by breaking them up into small pieces. When these traders suspect that buyers or sellers are working large orders, they will trade ahead on the same side with the hope of benefiting when the large traders move prices as they fill their orders. This front-running strategy is legal if the information on which it is based is properly obtained — for example, by watching a market data feed. Front running increases transaction costs for the traders whose orders are front run.'

options.A: the dissemination of false information about fundamental values or about other traders' trading intentions to alter investors' value assessments.
options.B: Rumormongering is always legal because opinions about securities are protected speech.
options.C: Rumormongering is not a practice recognized in market-abuse taxonomies.

rationales.A:
Full statement: the dissemination of false information about fundamental values or about other traders' trading intentions to alter investors' value assessments; illegal in most jurisdictions; analysts must ensure they base analyses on valid, balanced information, not false or one-sided information designed to distort analyses.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Rumormongering is the dissemination of false information about fundamental values or about other traders' trading intentions to alter investors' value assessments. Financial analysts must be careful to ensure that they base their analyses on valid information and not on false information designed to fool them into making poor decisions. Note that although rumormongering is illegal in most jurisdictions, simply reporting one side of an issue is not illegal. Financial analysts, therefore, must also be careful to ensure that they base their analyses on balanced information and not on information that is true but selectively presented to them with the purpose of distorting their analyses.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Disseminating FALSE information to move valuations is market manipulation, not protected opinion.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The practice is a recognized abuse — false claims about values or other traders' intentions.

options.A: legitimate trades.
options.B: trades arranged among commonly controlled accounts to create the impression of market activity at a particular price.
options.C: no such practice.

rationales.A: Deceptive.
rationales.B:
Full statement: trades arranged among commonly controlled accounts to create the impression of market activity at a particular price; purpose is to fool investors into believing market is more liquid than it truly is and to increase investors' confidence in ability to exit positions and in security value assessments.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Wash trading consists of trades arranged among commonly controlled accounts to create the impression of market activity at a particular price. The purpose of wash trading is to fool investors into believing that a market is more liquid than it truly is and to thereby increase investors' confidence both in their ability to exit positions without substantial cost and in their assessments of security values. Manipulators also can achieve these purposes by falsely reporting trades that never occurred, which is essentially what happens when they arrange trades among commonly controlled accounts.'
rationales.C: Illegal practice.

options.A: Spoofing is a hidden-order strategy that conceals trading intentions from the market.
options.B: Spoofing is not a recognized practice in electronic markets.
options.C: traders placing exposed standing limit orders to convey impression to other traders that the market is more liquid than it is or to suggest security is under- or overvalued.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Spoofing uses EXPOSED standing limit orders — visibility is the mechanism of deception.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Spoofing/layering is a recognized and prosecuted abuse: fake displayed liquidity to mislead other traders.
rationales.C:
Full statement: traders placing exposed standing limit orders to convey impression to other traders that the market is more liquid than it is or to suggest security is under- or overvalued; example: spoofer places hidden buy order + exposed sell limit orders to convey price may fall; some traders sell into spoofer's buy order; spoofer then cancels sell orders.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Spoofing, also known as layering, is a trading practice in which traders place exposed standing limit orders to convey an impression to other traders that the market is more liquid than it is or to suggest to other traders that the security is under- or overvalued. For example, suppose that a spoofer wants to buy stock cheaply or quickly. The spoofer might place a hidden buy order in the market. The spoofer then places one or more exposed sell limit orders in the market to convey the impression that prices may soon fall. Seeing the spoofing sell orders, one or more traders may conclude that values may be lower than market prices suggest. On that basis, they may sell into the spoofer's buy order, enabling the spoofer to obtain a quick and possibly cheaper purchase.'

options.A: submitting orders and arranging trades to influence other traders' perceptions of value.
options.B: no manipulation.
options.C: always legitimate.

rationales.A:
Full statement: submitting orders and arranging trades to influence other traders' perceptions of value; bluffers often prey on momentum traders (buy when prices are rising, sell when falling); example: bluffers buy stock to raise price and encourage momentum traders to buy, then sell stock to them at higher prices; may engage in rumormongering or wash trading to further scheme.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Bluffing. Bluffing involves submitting orders and arranging trades to influence other traders' perceptions of value. Bluffers often prey on momentum traders, who buy when prices are rising and sell when prices are falling. For example, consider typical "pump-and-dump" schemes in which bluffers buy stock to raise its price and thereby encourage momentum traders to buy. The bluffers then sell the stock to the momentum traders at higher prices. To further the scheme, bluffers may engage in such activities as rumormongering or wash trading. Note also that bluffers may time their purchases to immediately follow the release of valid positive information about the security and thereby fool traders into overvaluing the material significance of the new information.' Similar short-side manipulations are called 'short and distorts'.
rationales.B: Manipulative.
rationales.C: Illegal.

options.A: Gunning the market is not a recognized manipulation strategy.
options.B: strategy used by market manipulators to force traders to do disadvantageous trades — generally by selling quickly to push prices down with hope of triggering stop-loss sell orders.
options.C: Gunning the market is a legitimate strategy for providing liquidity in stressed conditions.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The tactic is recognized manipulation.
rationales.B:
Full statement: strategy used by market manipulators to force traders to do disadvantageous trades — generally by selling quickly to push prices down with hope of triggering stop-loss sell orders; e.g., manipulator sells aggressively to push prices from 51 to 50 to trigger stop-loss sells clustered at 50, then repurchases at lower prices.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Gunning the market. Gunning the market is a strategy used by market manipulators to force traders to do disadvantageous trades. A manipulator generally guns the market by selling quickly to push prices down with the hope of triggering stop-loss sell orders. A stop-loss (or stop) sell order becomes valid for execution once the specified stop price condition is met by a trade occurring at or below the stop price. For example, suppose that a market manipulator believes that traders have placed many stop-loss sell orders at 50. These sell orders would become valid upon a trade occurring at 50 or below. The manipulator may sell aggressively to push prices down from 51 to 50 and thereby trigger the stop-loss sell orders. The manipulator then may be able to profit by repurchasing at lower prices.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Forcing disadvantageous trades — selling fast to push prices through stop levels — is abuse, not liquidity provision.

options.A: Squeezing and cornering are not practices that occur in modern markets, having been eliminated by position limits and electronic surveillance of deliverable contracts.
options.B: Squeezing and cornering are always legal expressions of market power, protected as legitimate accumulation of scarce assets by traders with superior foresight.
options.C: schemes to force traders to do disadvantageous trades — manipulator obtains control over resources necessary to settle trading contracts, then unexpectedly withdraws those resources from market causing traders to default on contracts (some of which manipulator may hold).

rationales.A: Incorrect. Both schemes persist, particularly around deliverable contracts.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Controlling the resources needed to settle contracts in order to force disadvantageous trades is illegal manipulation.
rationales.C:
Full statement: schemes to force traders to do disadvantageous trades — manipulator obtains control over resources necessary to settle trading contracts, then unexpectedly withdraws those resources from market causing traders to default on contracts (some of which manipulator may hold); short squeezes: manipulators control substantial fraction of lendable shares, then recall stock loans; commodity corners: manipulators buy many futures contracts + deliverable supply in spot; illegal in most jurisdictions.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Squeezing and cornering. Squeezing, cornering, and gunning the market are all schemes that market manipulators use to force traders to do disadvantageous trades. In a squeeze or corner, the manipulator obtains control over resources necessary to settle trading contracts. The manipulator then unexpectedly withdraws those resources from the market, which causes traders to default on their contracts, some of which the manipulator may hold... For example, in short squeezes, manipulators obtain control of a substantial fraction of all available lendable stock shares or bonds. If the securities are overvalued... many speculators may be short selling the securities by unknowingly borrowing them from the manipulators. The manipulators then will recall the security loans. If the short sellers ("shorts") cannot borrow the securities from others, they will be forced to buy securities in the market to cover their stock loans... In commodity market corners, manipulators buy many futures contracts while simultaneously buying in the spot markets much of the deliverable supply of the commodity. Corners are illegal in most jurisdictions.'
